subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Summary Conviction Appeals — s. 813 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: The accused is convicted of a summary conviction offence in the Ontario Court of Justice. He wishes to appeal the conviction on the basis that the trial judge misapplied the law.
call_of_question: Under s. 813 of the Criminal Code, to which court does the accused appeal from a summary conviction?

options.A: The Court of Appeal for Ontario.
options.B: The Superior Court of Justice (as a summary conviction appeal court).
options.D: The Divisional Court, which has exclusive jurisdiction over all criminal appeals.

explanation: Section 813 of the Criminal Code provides that the defendant or the informant may appeal to the summary conviction appeal court against a conviction, acquittal, or sentence. In Ontario, the summary conviction appeal court is the Superior Court of Justice (pursuant to the provincial designation). The appeal from a summary conviction goes first to the Superior Court of Justice, not to the Court of Appeal. A further appeal on a question of law lies to the Court of Appeal for Ontario with leave, under s. 839 of the Criminal Code.
why_A_wrong: The Court of Appeal is not the first-level appeal court for summary convictions. Summary conviction appeals go to the summary conviction appeal court (Superior Court of Justice in Ontario). The Court of Appeal hears further appeals on questions of law with leave under s. 839.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 813, the summary conviction appeal court in Ontario is the Superior Court of Justice.
why_C_wrong: The Supreme Court of Canada is not a first-level appeal court for summary convictions. It hears appeals only on questions of law of public importance with leave, typically after the Court of Appeal has considered the matter.
why_D_wrong: The Divisional Court does not have jurisdiction over criminal appeals. The Divisional Court handles civil appeals and judicial review of administrative decisions. Criminal summary conviction appeals go to the Superior Court of Justice acting as a summary conviction appeal court.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Summary Conviction Appeals — s. 813
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 813, s. 839

tested_concepts: s. 813 summary conviction appeal court
Superior Court of Justice as appeal court for summary convictions
further appeal to Court of Appeal (s. 839)

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Indictable Offence Appeals — s. 675 and s. 676
fact_pattern: The accused is convicted of robbery (an indictable offence) after a jury trial. He seeks to appeal his conviction to the Court of Appeal for Ontario.
call_of_question: Under s. 675 of the Criminal Code, on what grounds may the accused appeal a conviction entered after a jury trial?

options.A: On any ground, including both questions of law and questions of fact, as of right.
options.B: On a question of law as of right; on a question of fact or mixed fact and law only with leave of the Court of Appeal.
options.C: On a question of law only, with leave of the Court of Appeal; no appeal on questions of fact is available.
options.D: Only if the sentence imposed was manifestly excessive.

explanation: Section 675(1) of the Criminal Code provides that a person convicted of an indictable offence may appeal to the Court of Appeal: (a) on any ground of appeal that involves a question of law alone, as of right; (b) on any ground of appeal that involves a question of fact alone, or a question of mixed law and fact, with leave of the Court of Appeal or on the certification of the trial judge; and (c) on any ground that appears to the Court of Appeal to be a sufficient ground, with leave. After a jury trial, s.675(1.1) limits grounds to: question of law as of right; question of fact with leave. The fundamental structure is: questions of law — as of right; questions of fact — with leave.
why_A_wrong: An appeal on questions of fact is not available as of right under s.675. It requires leave of the Court of Appeal. The as-of-right appeal is limited to questions of law alone.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 675(1) permits appeals on questions of law as of right, and on questions of fact or mixed fact and law with leave of the Court of Appeal.
why_C_wrong: Appeals on questions of fact are not categorically barred under s.675 — they require leave but are available. The statement that 'no appeal on questions of fact is available' is incorrect.
why_D_wrong: Section 675 does not limit appeals to cases of manifestly excessive sentences. Sentence appeals are governed by s.675(1)(b.1), which permits sentence appeals with leave on grounds of sentence only. The conviction appeal grounds include questions of law and (with leave) questions of fact.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Indictable Conviction Appeal — s. 675
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 675(1)

tested_concepts: s. 675 indictable appeal grounds
questions of law as of right
questions of fact with leave
jury trial appeal grounds

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test
fact_pattern: The accused was convicted of fraud at trial. On appeal, he seeks to introduce new affidavit evidence from a forensic accountant who was retained after the trial. The forensic accountant's opinion contradicts the Crown's expert evidence at trial. The accused did not call an expert accountant at trial even though defence counsel was aware such evidence might be helpful.
call_of_question: Under the Palmer test for fresh evidence on criminal appeals, which requirement is MOST likely to defeat the accused's application to introduce this evidence?

options.A: The evidence must be credible in the sense that it is reasonably capable of belief.
options.B: The evidence must be relevant and bear on a decisive or potentially decisive issue.
options.C: The evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence at trial — the due diligence requirement.
options.D: The evidence must, if believed, have affected the verdict.

explanation: In Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759, the Supreme Court set out the four-part test for admitting fresh evidence on appeal: (1) the evidence could not, by the exercise of reasonable diligence, have been obtained for use at trial (due diligence); (2) the evidence must be relevant in the sense that it bears on a decisive or potentially decisive issue; (3) the evidence must be credible in the sense that it is reasonably capable of belief; and (4) the evidence, if believed, could reasonably be expected to have affected the result. On these facts, the due diligence requirement is the most problematic criterion. Defence counsel was aware of the potential need for a forensic accountant but did not retain one. The failure to call available expert evidence at trial is a strategic choice that the accused cannot revisit on appeal under the guise of fresh evidence.
why_A_wrong: Credibility (requirement 3 of Palmer) is not the most challenging requirement on these facts. The forensic accountant's evidence would likely be credible as expert opinion. The failing is the due diligence requirement, not credibility.
why_B_wrong: Relevance (requirement 2 of Palmer) is not the most challenging requirement here. Evidence that directly contradicts the Crown's expert on the key issue is clearly relevant. The problem is due diligence — the evidence was available at trial.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The due diligence requirement is the most likely defeat for this fresh evidence application because defence counsel was aware of the potential value of expert accounting evidence but chose not to retain one at trial. This defeats the 'could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence' requirement.
why_D_wrong: Whether the evidence could have affected the verdict (requirement 4) is not the most challenging criterion. Contradicting the Crown's expert on fraud accounting could well affect the verdict. The due diligence failure is the primary bar.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759; Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 683(1)(d)

tested_concepts: Palmer four-part fresh evidence test
due diligence requirement
strategic choice vs fresh evidence
credibility and relevance of fresh evidence

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The accused is acquitted by a jury of first degree murder after the trial judge incorrectly directed the jury on the law of mens rea. The Crown wishes to appeal the acquittal.
call_of_question: Under s. 676 of the Criminal Code, on what grounds may the Crown appeal an acquittal by a jury?

options.A: The Crown may appeal on any ground — questions of law, fact, or mixed fact and law — as of right.
options.B: The Crown may appeal only on a question of law, as of right.
options.C: The Crown may not appeal a jury acquittal in Canada because of the constitutional prohibition against double jeopardy.
options.D: The Crown may appeal on any ground with leave of the Court of Appeal, including questions of fact.

explanation: Section 676(1) of the Criminal Code provides that the Attorney General or counsel acting on behalf of the Attorney General may appeal against a verdict of acquittal or a judgment in favour of the accused on any ground of appeal that involves a question of law alone. The Crown's right to appeal an acquittal is significantly more restricted than the accused's appeal rights. The Crown may appeal a jury acquittal only on a question of law (including a legal error in the trial judge's charge to the jury), and this is an as-of-right appeal. The Crown cannot appeal on questions of fact, mixed fact and law, or on the ground that the verdict was unreasonable. If the Crown's appeal is successful, the Court of Appeal may set aside the acquittal and enter a conviction, order a new trial, or make any other order that justice requires (s. 686(4)).
why_A_wrong: The Crown's appeal rights under s.676 are more limited than the accused's. The Crown may not appeal on questions of fact or mixed fact and law from a jury acquittal. The Crown's appeal is restricted to questions of law alone.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s.676(1), the Crown may appeal a jury acquittal on a question of law alone, as of right.
why_C_wrong: The Charter's s.11(h) protection against double jeopardy is not an absolute bar to Crown appeals in Canada. The Criminal Code expressly provides for Crown appeals against acquittals on questions of law (s.676), and the SCC has upheld this as consistent with the Charter.
why_D_wrong: The Crown does not need leave to appeal a jury acquittal on a question of law — it is an as-of-right appeal. However, the Crown cannot appeal on questions of fact even with leave. The Crown's appeal right is limited to questions of law alone.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Appeal of Acquittal — s. 676
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1), s. 686(4); Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 11(h)

tested_concepts: s. 676 Crown appeal of acquittal
questions of law only for Crown
as-of-right Crown appeal on law
distinction from accused appeal rights
s. 11(h) Charter double jeopardy

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Court of Appeal Powers on Indictable Conviction Appeal — s. 686
fact_pattern: The accused was convicted of assault with a weapon after a judge-alone trial. On appeal, the Court of Appeal finds that the trial judge made a legal error in admitting a statement that should have been excluded under the Charter. The error was not harmless — without the statement, a conviction could not have been sustained.
call_of_question: Under s. 686(2) of the Criminal Code, which disposition is available to the Court of Appeal where it allows the accused's appeal?

options.A: The Court of Appeal may only set aside the conviction and order a new trial; it has no power to enter an acquittal.
options.B: The Court of Appeal may set aside the conviction and either order a new trial or, where appropriate, enter a verdict of acquittal.
options.C: The Court of Appeal must set aside the conviction and enter a verdict of acquittal whenever a Charter breach is found.
options.D: The Court of Appeal may reduce the sentence but may not set aside the conviction on a legal error.

explanation: Section 686(2) of the Criminal Code provides that where the Court of Appeal allows an appeal against a conviction, it shall quash the conviction and: (a) direct a judgment or verdict of acquittal to be entered; or (b) order a new trial. The choice between acquittal and a new trial depends on the nature of the error. Where the evidence, properly admitted, is legally insufficient to sustain any conviction, an acquittal is entered. Where the error is one that, if corrected, could have led to conviction on a proper retrial (e.g., the error tainted one piece of evidence but other evidence remains), a new trial is the appropriate remedy. On these facts, since without the excluded statement a conviction could not be sustained, directing an acquittal would be appropriate. A new trial would also be available if some other theory of conviction remained viable.
why_A_wrong: The Court of Appeal does have the power to enter an acquittal under s.686(2)(a). It is not limited to ordering a new trial. The choice depends on whether the remaining admissible evidence could support a conviction.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s.686(2), the Court of Appeal may set aside the conviction and either enter a verdict of acquittal (where evidence is insufficient) or order a new trial (where a properly conducted retrial could yield a conviction).
why_C_wrong: Finding a Charter breach does not automatically mandate acquittal. The remedy for a Charter breach at trial is determined under s.24 of the Charter. On appeal, even where a Charter-tainted piece of evidence was wrongly admitted, the Court of Appeal must assess whether the remaining admissible evidence could sustain a conviction. An acquittal is entered only where it cannot.
why_D_wrong: The Court of Appeal has broad powers on a conviction appeal beyond sentence reduction. Where it finds reversible legal error, it must quash the conviction and order either acquittal or a new trial under s.686(2). Reducing the sentence without addressing the conviction would not be the correct disposition after finding reversible error on conviction.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Court of Appeal Disposition Powers — s. 686
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 686(2)

tested_concepts: s.686(2) disposition options — acquittal or new trial
entry of acquittal vs new trial
reversible legal error on appeal
Charter error and appellate remedy

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The accused pleads guilty to sexual assault and is sentenced to a conditional sentence order of 18 months in the community. The Crown, believing the sentence is demonstrably unfit, seeks to appeal the sentence to the Court of Appeal.
call_of_question: Under s. 676(1)(d) of the Criminal Code, on what basis may the Crown appeal a sentence imposed at trial?

options.A: The Crown may appeal a sentence only if the trial judge committed an error of law in the sentencing principles applied.
options.B: The Crown may appeal a sentence with leave of the Court of Appeal, on the ground that the sentence is inadequate.
options.C: The Crown may appeal a sentence as of right, without leave, whenever the Crown considers the sentence manifestly inadequate.
options.D: The Crown has no right to appeal a sentence imposed following a guilty plea.

explanation: Section 676(1)(d) of the Criminal Code provides that the Attorney General may appeal to the Court of Appeal against the sentence passed by a trial court, with leave of the Court of Appeal, unless the sentence is one fixed by law. On a Crown sentence appeal, the Court of Appeal applies the standard of review from R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64: the Court of Appeal may intervene only if the sentence is demonstrably unfit or the trial judge committed an error in principle that had an impact on the sentence. The ground for a Crown sentence appeal is that the sentence is 'inadequate' from the Crown's perspective — essentially, demonstrably unfit on the low end. Leave is required for Crown sentence appeals, unlike the Crown's as-of-right appeal on questions of law from acquittals (s.676(1)(a)).
why_A_wrong: Crown sentence appeals are not limited to legal errors in sentencing principles. The Crown may appeal on the ground that the sentence is inadequate (demonstrably unfit), which can include cases where the trial judge exercised proper legal principles but the resulting sentence is still outside the range. Leave is required either way.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s.676(1)(d), the Crown may appeal a sentence with leave of the Court of Appeal on the ground that the sentence is inadequate.
why_C_wrong: Crown sentence appeals require leave — they are not as-of-right appeals. The Crown's as-of-right appeal is limited to questions of law from acquittals (s.676(1)(a)). A Crown sentence appeal always requires leave under s.676(1)(d).
why_D_wrong: The Crown's right to appeal sentence under s.676(1)(d) is not limited by the mode of conviction. A sentence following a guilty plea can be appealed by the Crown just as one following a contested trial, subject to obtaining leave and demonstrating the sentence is inadequate.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Crown Sentence Appeal — s. 676(1)(d)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1)(d); R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64

tested_concepts: s.676(1)(d) Crown sentence appeal
leave required for Crown sentence appeal
demonstrably unfit standard
distinction from as-of-right acquittal appeal

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The accused is convicted of robbery after a jury trial. On appeal, the Court of Appeal finds that the trial judge made a legal error in instructing the jury on the defence of honest but mistaken belief. However, the evidence of the accused's guilt was overwhelming — numerous eyewitnesses and DNA evidence directly implicated the accused. The Court of Appeal considers whether to apply the curative proviso.
call_of_question: Under s. 686(1)(b)(iii) of the Criminal Code, in which circumstances may the Court of Appeal dismiss an appeal despite finding that the trial judge made an error of law?

options.A: Where the accused consents to dismissal of the appeal and waives the right to a new trial.
options.B: Where no substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice occurred as a result of the error — the curative proviso applies to harmless legal errors.
options.C: Where the Crown establishes that the accused would have been convicted on the same charges even without the error.
options.D: Where the error was procedural rather than substantive.

explanation: Section 686(1)(b)(iii) of the Criminal Code — the 'curative proviso' — provides that the Court of Appeal may dismiss an appeal from conviction where, despite finding that there was an error of law at trial, the Court is of the opinion that no substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice occurred. The test for applying the proviso was clarified in R v Khan, 2001 SCC 86, and R v Bevan, [1993] 2 SCR 599: the proviso applies in two situations: (i) where the error was harmless in the sense that it could not have affected the verdict (minor procedural irregularity); or (ii) where the evidence of guilt was so overwhelming that any reasonable and properly instructed jury would inevitably have convicted. On these facts, with overwhelming eyewitness and DNA evidence, the second branch (overwhelming evidence) of the proviso would likely apply, allowing the Court of Appeal to dismiss the appeal despite the jury instruction error.
why_A_wrong: Accused consent is not part of the s.686(1)(b)(iii) proviso analysis. The curative proviso is applied by the Court of Appeal based on the nature of the error and its effect on the verdict — it does not depend on the accused's consent or waiver.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The curative proviso under s.686(1)(b)(iii) permits dismissal of the appeal where the legal error caused no substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice — either because the error was harmless or because the evidence of guilt was overwhelming.
why_C_wrong: The standard is not whether the Crown can establish actual certainty of conviction without the error. The Court of Appeal applies the proviso where no substantial wrong occurred — which may be established by overwhelming evidence, but the framing is whether a reasonable jury would inevitably have convicted, not a retrospective factual determination by the Crown.
why_D_wrong: The distinction between procedural and substantive errors does not determine whether the proviso applies. The curative proviso can apply to substantive legal errors (including errors in jury charges on elements of the offence) if the error caused no substantial wrong. The nature of the error as procedural or substantive is relevant but is not the statutory test.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Curative Proviso — s. 686(1)(b)(iii)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 686(1)(b)(iii); R v Khan, 2001 SCC 86; R v Bevan, [1993] 2 SCR 599

tested_concepts: s.686(1)(b)(iii) curative proviso
two branches: harmless error and overwhelming evidence
substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice
Khan/Bevan test for proviso application

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Error of Law vs. Mixed Fact and Law on Appeal
fact_pattern: The accused was acquitted of impaired driving. The trial judge, after finding the accused's blood alcohol level to be 0.092 at the time of testing, concluded that this was insufficient to establish impairment at the time of driving. The Crown appeals, arguing the trial judge erred in assessing the evidence. The accused argues this is a finding of fact unreviewable by the Crown on appeal.
call_of_question: The Crown's appeal is properly frounded as a question of law if which of the following is true?

options.A: The trial judge reached a conclusion on the evidence that another judge might reasonably have reached differently.
options.B: The trial judge applied a legal standard requiring the Crown to prove impairment at a threshold beyond what the law requires, constituting a legal error in the standard of proof.
options.C: The trial judge made credibility findings that the Court of Appeal considers unreasonable.
options.D: The trial judge failed to give reasons for rejecting the Crown's expert evidence on back-calculation.

explanation: The distinction between a question of law and a question of fact (or mixed fact and law) is critical to Crown appeals under s.676(1)(a), which restricts the Crown to questions of law alone. A 'question of law' on appeal includes: errors in the legal standard applied, errors in the legal test for an element of an offence, errors in the burden of proof, and unreasonable verdicts (in some formulations). A 'question of fact' is a finding about what happened — it is not reviewable by the Crown. On these facts, if the trial judge incorrectly applied the legal threshold for impairment — requiring proof to a standard higher than required by law — that is a question of law. For example, if the trial judge incorrectly concluded that 0.092 BAC could not, as a matter of law, constitute impairment, that is a legal error. By contrast, if the judge simply weighed the evidence and was not satisfied beyond a reasonable doubt, that is a question of fact not open to Crown appeal.
why_A_wrong: The fact that another judge might have reached a different conclusion is not a question of law — it describes a question of fact or credibility, which is within the exclusive province of the trier of fact. The Crown cannot appeal a jury or judge's factual assessment simply because the outcome was different from what the Crown expected.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. If the trial judge applied a legal standard for impairment that was incorrect — requiring proof to a higher threshold than the law demands — that is a question of law reviewable by the Crown under s.676(1)(a). Applying the wrong legal standard is a legal error.
why_C_wrong: Credibility findings are quintessential questions of fact. The Crown cannot appeal an acquittal on the basis that the trial judge's credibility assessments were unreasonable (except in the limited context of an unreasonable verdict, which has its own analysis). Attacking credibility findings as such is not a question of law for Crown appeal purposes.
why_D_wrong: The failure to give reasons for rejecting evidence may in some cases constitute a legal error (e.g., where the reasons are so inadequate they prevent meaningful appellate review), but this is a more attenuated legal error. The clearest and most direct answer is B — applying the wrong legal standard is unambiguously a question of law.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Error of Law vs. Mixed Fact and Law — Crown Appeal
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 676(1)(a); R v Biniaris, 2000 SCC 15

tested_concepts: distinction between question of law and question of fact
Crown restricted to questions of law on acquittal appeal
incorrect legal standard as question of law
credibility findings as questions of fact

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Accused Sentence Appeal — Standard of Review — R v Lacasse
fact_pattern: The accused is convicted of impaired driving causing death and sentenced to eight years' imprisonment. He appeals his sentence to the Court of Appeal, arguing that eight years is outside the appropriate range for such an offence in comparable cases and that the sentencing judge placed too much weight on denunciation at the expense of his prospects for rehabilitation.
call_of_question: Under the standard of appellate review for sentence appeals established in R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64, on what basis may the Court of Appeal intervene in a sentence imposed by a trial judge?

options.A: The Court of Appeal may intervene whenever the sentence differs from the median sentence in comparable cases by more than 20%.
options.B: The Court of Appeal may substitute its own sentence whenever it disagrees with the weight the trial judge assigned to the sentencing principles.
options.C: The Court of Appeal may intervene only if the sentencing judge committed an error in principle that had an impact on the sentence, or if the sentence is demonstrably unfit.
options.D: The Court of Appeal has a de novo sentencing function and is not bound by deference to the trial judge's sentencing discretion.

explanation: In R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64, the Supreme Court affirmed that sentencing judges have broad discretion and that courts of appeal should show deference to trial judges' sentencing decisions. The Court of Appeal may intervene in a sentence only in two circumstances: (1) where the sentencing judge committed an error in principle — including an error in the applicable legal principles, failure to consider a relevant factor, or erroneous consideration of an aggravating or mitigating factor — AND that error had an impact on the sentence; or (2) where the sentence, even absent an identifiable error, is demonstrably unfit. A mere disagreement with the weight assigned to sentencing principles or factors is not a basis for appellate intervention — the sentencing judge's weighing is entitled to deference within the proper range.
why_A_wrong: There is no percentage-deviation threshold for appellate sentence review. The Lacasse standard requires either an error in principle with impact on the sentence, or a demonstrably unfit sentence — not a mechanical comparison to median sentences in comparable cases.
why_B_wrong: Disagreement with how the trial judge weighted sentencing principles is not a sufficient basis for appellate intervention under Lacasse. The appellate court must identify an error in principle or a demonstrably unfit sentence. Reweighing factors is precisely the kind of appellate interference that Lacasse cautions against.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under R v Lacasse, the Court of Appeal may intervene only if there was an error in principle that had an impact on the sentence, or if the sentence is demonstrably unfit.
why_D_wrong: Courts of appeal do not have a de novo sentencing function. Lacasse affirms that deference is owed to trial judges' sentencing discretion because they are best positioned to assess the offence, offender, and community context. Appellate courts act in a supervisory, not substitutional, capacity.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Accused Sentence Appeal — Standard of Review
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 675(1)(b.1); R v Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64

tested_concepts: Lacasse two-part test: error in principle with impact OR demonstrably unfit
appellate deference to trial judge on sentencing
disagreement with weighing is not error in principle
demonstrably unfit as alternative basis for intervention

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Release Pending Appeal — s. 679 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: The accused is convicted of fraud over $5,000 and sentenced to two years less a day, to be served in the community as a conditional sentence. The Crown successfully argues that the trial judge erred. The Court of Appeal allows the Crown's sentence appeal and substitutes a two-year federal penitentiary sentence. The accused wishes to remain out of custody while he seeks leave to appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada.
call_of_question: Under s. 679(1)(b) of the Criminal Code, what must an accused establish to obtain a release order pending an appeal to the Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court of Canada?

options.A: The accused must demonstrate that the appeal raises a novel question of law not previously decided by the Supreme Court of Canada.
options.B: The accused must show that the appeal is not frivolous, that he will surrender into custody in accordance with the order, and that detention is not necessary in the public interest.
options.C: The accused must post sufficient surety to guarantee appearance and demonstrate a reasonable prospect of success on the appeal.
options.D: The accused need only show that the sentence is manifestly excessive and that an immediate appeal has been filed.

explanation: Section 679(3) of the Criminal Code sets out the three-part test for release pending a conviction or sentence appeal. The judge of the Court of Appeal (or SCC) shall order that the accused be released if the accused establishes: (a) that the appeal or application for leave to appeal is not frivolous; (b) that the accused will surrender into custody in accordance with the terms of the order; and (c) that the detention of the accused is not necessary in the public interest. The 'public interest' component in (c) involves balancing the reviewability of the decision (which informs the need to review before enforcement) against the enforceability interest (the public interest in immediate execution of lawful sentences). The accused bears the burden of satisfying all three criteria under s. 679(3).
why_A_wrong: No requirement exists that the appeal raise a novel question of law or an SCC-level issue. The test under s. 679(3) is whether the appeal is not frivolous (a low threshold — not patently without merit), not whether it raises an issue of general importance.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 679(3) requires the accused to establish: (a) appeal not frivolous; (b) will surrender as ordered; and (c) detention not necessary in the public interest. All three must be satisfied.
why_C_wrong: Posting surety may be a condition of release, but it is not one of the three statutory tests under s. 679(3). Demonstrating a reasonable prospect of success is not the standard — the standard is that the appeal is 'not frivolous,' which is a lower threshold than a reasonable prospect of success.
why_D_wrong: Demonstrating a manifestly excessive sentence is not the statutory test under s. 679(3). The three-part test applies regardless of the type of appeal (conviction or sentence). Filing an appeal is a precondition to applying for release pending appeal, not a standalone basis for release.
tested_concepts: s. 679(3) three-part test: not frivolous, surrender, public interest
accused bears burden on release pending appeal
not frivolous as low threshold
public interest balancing test

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Appeals to the Supreme Court of Canada — s. 691 and s. 40 SCA
fact_pattern: The accused was convicted of robbery and his appeal was dismissed by a panel of five judges of the Court of Appeal, with two judges dissenting on a question of law. He wishes to further appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada.
call_of_question: Under s. 691(2) of the Criminal Code, does the accused have an as-of-right appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada in these circumstances, and if so, on what basis?

options.A: No — an accused may only appeal to the SCC by obtaining leave to appeal on a question of public importance under s. 40 of the Supreme Court Act.
options.B: Yes — a dissent in the Court of Appeal on any question creates an as-of-right appeal to the SCC regardless of whether the dissent is on a question of law.
options.C: Yes — under s. 691(2), where a judge of the Court of Appeal dissents on a question of law, the accused has an as-of-right appeal to the SCC on that question of law.
options.D: No — as-of-right appeals to the SCC from criminal convictions were abolished; all criminal appeals to the SCC require leave.

explanation: Section 691(2) of the Criminal Code provides that a person who is convicted of an indictable offence and whose conviction is affirmed by the Court of Appeal may appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada without leave, as of right, if a judge of the Court of Appeal dissented on a question of law. The as-of-right appeal under s. 691(2) is limited to the question(s) of law on which the dissent occurred. This is a significant jurisdictional provision — a single dissenting judge on a question of law opens the door to the Supreme Court of Canada without the need for leave. By contrast, where there is no dissent on a question of law, the accused must seek leave under s. 40 of the Supreme Court Act on the basis that the appeal involves a question of public importance.
why_A_wrong: Leave under s. 40 of the Supreme Court Act is not the only route to the SCC in criminal cases. Section 691(2) of the Criminal Code provides an as-of-right appeal where a Court of Appeal judge dissents on a question of law. The accused in this fact pattern has a dissent on a question of law, creating an as-of-right appeal.
why_B_wrong: The as-of-right appeal under s. 691(2) requires that the dissent be on a question of law — a dissent on a question of fact or a mixed question without a legal component does not trigger the as-of-right appeal. The question of law requirement is essential.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 691(2) gives an accused an as-of-right appeal to the SCC where a Court of Appeal judge dissented on a question of law. The appeal is limited to that question of law.
why_D_wrong: As-of-right criminal appeals to the SCC have not been abolished. Section 691(2) preserves the as-of-right appeal based on a dissent on a question of law. Leave-based appeals under s. 40 SCA are a parallel (and more common) route, but they have not replaced the s. 691(2) as-of-right mechanism.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Supreme Court of Canada Appeal — s. 691
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 691(2); Supreme Court Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. S-26, s. 40

tested_concepts: s. 691(2) as-of-right SCC appeal on dissent on question of law
dissent must be on question of law
s. 40 SCA leave requirement where no dissent
distinction between as-of-right and leave-based SCC appeals

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Summary Conviction Appeal — Sentence Appeal Jurisdiction
fact_pattern: The accused is convicted in the Ontario Court of Justice of a summary conviction offence and sentenced to 60 days' intermittent custody. She believes the sentence is excessive and wishes to appeal.
call_of_question: Under s. 813 of the Criminal Code, may the accused appeal the sentence in a summary conviction matter, and is leave of the court required?

options.A: The accused may not appeal the sentence in a summary conviction matter — only the Crown may appeal a sentence in such proceedings.
options.B: The accused may appeal the sentence, but leave of the summary conviction appeal court is required.
options.C: The accused may appeal the sentence to the summary conviction appeal court as of right, without leave.
options.D: The accused must first apply to the Ontario Court of Justice to vary the sentence before appealing to the summary conviction appeal court.

explanation: Section 813(a) of the Criminal Code provides that the defendant in summary conviction proceedings may appeal to the summary conviction appeal court against the conviction, against the sentence, or against a verdict of not criminally responsible on account of mental disorder. The appeal is as of right — no leave is required for either a conviction appeal or a sentence appeal by the defendant under s. 813. In Ontario, the summary conviction appeal court is the Superior Court of Justice. This contrasts with the Crown's right to appeal a sentence under s. 676(1)(d) (indictable) which requires leave. Summary conviction sentence appeals by the defendant are as-of-right under s. 813.
why_A_wrong: Section 813(a) expressly provides the defendant with the right to appeal the sentence in summary conviction proceedings. Both the defendant and the informant (Crown) have appeal rights under s. 813. The defendant's right to appeal sentence is as of right.
why_B_wrong: No leave is required for a defendant to appeal sentence under s. 813. The appeal is as of right. This is one of the distinctions between summary conviction appeals (s. 813, as of right for defendant) and indictable sentence appeals (s. 675(1)(b.1), which may require leave).
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 813(a), the defendant may appeal the sentence in a summary conviction matter to the summary conviction appeal court as of right, without requiring leave.
why_D_wrong: There is no requirement to first apply to the trial court to vary the sentence before filing a summary conviction appeal. The defendant proceeds directly to the summary conviction appeal court under s. 813. No internal application to the originating court is required.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Summary Conviction Sentence Appeal — s. 813
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 813(a)

tested_concepts: s. 813(a) defendant's as-of-right summary conviction sentence appeal
no leave required for summary conviction sentence appeal by defendant
Superior Court of Justice as summary conviction appeal court in Ontario
summary conviction vs indictable appeal right to appeal sentence

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Unreasonable Verdict — s. 686(1)(a)(i) — R v Biniaris
fact_pattern: The accused was acquitted of sexual assault by a judge sitting alone, without a jury. The Crown appeals on the ground that the verdict was unreasonable under s. 686(1)(a)(i) of the Criminal Code. The trial judge provided detailed written reasons explaining why she found the complainant's evidence not credible based on specific inconsistencies she observed in the testimony.
call_of_question: Under the R v Biniaris, 2000 SCC 15 standard for unreasonable verdicts, which of the following best describes the approach the Court of Appeal must take when reviewing a judge-alone acquittal for unreasonableness?

options.A: The Court of Appeal applies the same test as for jury verdicts: would a properly instructed jury acting judicially have convicted on this evidence?
options.B: For a judge-alone verdict, the Court of Appeal reviews the reasoning in the written reasons to determine whether the verdict is the product of an illogical or legally erroneous reasoning process — engaging more deeply with the logic of the reasons than it would for a jury verdict.
options.C: An unreasonable verdict claim can only succeed if the trial judge made an explicit error of law in the written reasons — a bare credibility assessment cannot be challenged.
options.D: The Court of Appeal is required to assess the complainant's credibility de novo based on the trial record.

explanation: In R v Biniaris, 2000 SCC 15, the Supreme Court established the standard for unreasonable verdicts under s. 686(1)(a)(i). The test is whether the verdict is one that a properly instructed jury (or judge) acting judicially could reasonably have rendered. For jury verdicts, the appellate court must assess whether the evidence, if accepted, could support the verdict. For judge-alone verdicts, the appellate court has an additional tool: the written reasons. Where the trial judge provides reasons, the Court of Appeal may examine the logical flow of the reasoning and the legal correctness of the analysis. If the verdict is the product of an illogical or legally flawed reasoning process — even if the ultimate conclusion is not on its face unreasonable — the Court of Appeal may find the verdict unreasonable. This gives the appellate court deeper penetration into judge-alone verdicts than jury verdicts.
why_A_wrong: The test for judge-alone unreasonable verdicts is not identical to the jury test. For judge-alone verdicts, the appellate court has access to the reasoning through written reasons and can examine the logic of that reasoning more closely. The jury test — would a properly instructed jury have convicted — is adapted for judge-alone cases where reasons exist.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Biniaris allows the Court of Appeal to examine written reasons in judge-alone cases and assess whether the verdict is the product of illogical or legally erroneous reasoning — going beyond whether the conclusion is supported by evidence.
why_C_wrong: An unreasonable verdict claim is not limited to explicit errors of law in written reasons. The Biniaris test allows the Court of Appeal to find a verdict unreasonable based on the illogical reasoning process — including where the trial judge's assessment of credibility rests on legally flawed or internally inconsistent reasoning, even if framed as a 'credibility call.'
why_D_wrong: The Court of Appeal does not conduct a de novo assessment of credibility. The standard under Biniaris is whether the verdict could reasonably have been rendered — not whether the appellate court would have made the same credibility finding. Appellate courts are deferential to trial judges' credibility assessments and do not re-hear witnesses.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Unreasonable Verdict — s. 686(1)(a)(i)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 686(1)(a)(i); R v Biniaris, 2000 SCC 15

tested_concepts: Biniaris test for unreasonable verdict
judge-alone verdicts — review of written reasoning
jury vs judge-alone unreasonable verdict analysis
verdict is product of illogical or legally erroneous reasoning

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Habeas Corpus in Criminal Proceedings — s. 10(c) Charter
fact_pattern: The accused is serving a federal penitentiary sentence. He applies for habeas corpus to the Ontario Superior Court, claiming that prison authorities have placed him in administrative segregation without proper procedural safeguards, violating his rights under s. 7 of the Charter.
call_of_question: Which of the following correctly describes the availability of habeas corpus as a remedy in this situation?

options.A: Habeas corpus is unavailable to a federal inmate because the Federal Court has exclusive jurisdiction over challenges to federal penitentiary administration.
options.B: Habeas corpus is unavailable once a person has been lawfully convicted and sentenced — it can only be used to challenge pre-trial detention.
options.C: Habeas corpus is available in the Superior Court to challenge the legality of a change in the conditions of custody, such as administrative segregation, where the change amounts to a deprivation of residual liberty.
options.D: Habeas corpus is an extraordinary remedy available only after all statutory appeal rights have been exhausted.

explanation: Habeas corpus is a common law remedy preserved by s. 10(c) of the Charter (right to have the validity of detention determined). The Supreme Court has confirmed that provincial Superior Courts retain jurisdiction to hear habeas corpus applications from federal inmates — concurrent with the Federal Court — because habeas corpus is a fundamental supervisory jurisdiction: R v Miller, [1985] 2 SCR 613. Habeas corpus is available not only to challenge initial detention but also to challenge a change in the conditions of custody that constitutes a deprivation of residual liberty (e.g., placement in administrative segregation, transfer to a higher-security institution). A federal inmate who is placed in administrative segregation without procedural fairness suffers a change in conditions that may engage habeas corpus jurisdiction. The remedy is available even to convicted prisoners for challenges to the legality of the manner of their detention.
why_A_wrong: The Federal Court does not have exclusive jurisdiction over challenges to federal penitentiary administration. Provincial Superior Courts retain concurrent habeas corpus jurisdiction over federal inmates: Miller. The applicant may choose to proceed in the Superior Court or the Federal Court.
why_B_wrong: Habeas corpus is not limited to pre-trial detention. It is available to challenge the legality of any ongoing detention, including challenges by convicted persons to changes in the conditions of their custody that deprive them of residual liberty. Administrative segregation is a recognized basis for habeas corpus.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Following Miller, habeas corpus is available in the Superior Court to challenge a change in custodial conditions — such as administrative segregation — that constitutes a deprivation of residual liberty, even for a convicted federal inmate.
why_D_wrong: Habeas corpus does not require exhaustion of all statutory appeal rights. It is a distinct remedy that operates independently of the statutory appeal process. An inmate can apply for habeas corpus without first exhausting every other available avenue, though courts may decline to hear habeas corpus applications where adequate alternative remedies exist.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Habeas Corpus — Residual Liberty and Custodial Conditions
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(c); R v Miller, [1985] 2 SCR 613

tested_concepts: habeas corpus available to challenge conditions of custody
administrative segregation as deprivation of residual liberty
concurrent jurisdiction of Superior Court and Federal Court
habeas corpus not limited to pre-trial detention
